skills.name: Research Dossier
skills.summary: Turn scattered research inputs into a decision-ready dossier with evidence, themes, and unresolved questions.
triggers: You need to brief a team on a topic that spans many sources or interviews.
Raw research notes are piling up and decisions are getting delayed.
skills.description: Use when a team needs a structured research brief instead of raw notes, links, and loosely connected takeaways.
skills.problem: Teams often collect substantial research but fail to turn it into a concise artifact that can support actual decisions.
use_when: You need to brief a team on a topic that spans many sources or interviews.
You need a structured dossier with executive summary, evidence map, findings, tensions, and next-step recommendations instead of unstructured notes.
The workflow repeats often enough to justify a reusable asset.
avoid_when: You need original field research rather than synthesis from available materials.
The decision depends on live data that has not been collected yet.
skills.deliverable: A structured dossier with executive summary, evidence map, findings, tensions, and next-step recommendations

scenarios.title: Market entry briefing
scenarios.context: A strategy team needs a research packet on a new market before committing budget to a launch program.
scenarios.prompt: Synthesize analyst notes, competitor pricing snapshots, customer calls, and internal assumptions into a market-entry dossier.
expected: Separate facts, inferences, and assumptions.
Map the strongest signals and the biggest unknowns.
End with a clear recommendation on whether to proceed, pause, or research further.

scenarios.title: Interview-heavy synthesis
scenarios.context: A product team ran a dozen interviews and now needs a single artifact leadership can review quickly.
scenarios.prompt: Create a dossier from the interview notes, call recordings, and prior product metrics.
expected: Cluster recurring themes without erasing disagreement.
Show which findings are well supported and which are tentative.
Translate the findings into product or GTM implications.

scenarios.title: Urgent board prep
scenarios.context: Leadership needs a two-level output: an executive summary for the board and deeper evidence for staff follow-up.
scenarios.prompt: Produce a board-ready research dossier that keeps the short version crisp while preserving evidence quality underneath.
expected: Start with the key message for the board.
Keep the evidence traceable.
List the next questions that require more research before acting.

rubric.strong: Uses credible evidence, distinguishes primary and secondary material, and flags weak sourcing.
rubric.weak: Treats all sources as equal or cites unsupported assertions.

rubric.strong: Connects evidence into themes, tradeoffs, and decision-relevant insights.
rubric.weak: Restates findings without explaining why they matter.

rubric.strong: Ends with a brief that helps a team decide what to do next.
rubric.weak: Leaves the reader with a pile of notes and no recommendation.

edge_cases.title: Mixed source quality
edge_cases.risk: The available materials mix strong primary evidence with noisy secondary commentary.
edge_cases.response: Tier the evidence, reduce confidence where sourcing is thin, and avoid presenting weak commentary as established fact.

edge_cases.title: Contradictory signals
edge_cases.risk: Interviews and market data point in different directions.
edge_cases.response: Show the conflict, suggest possible explanations, and avoid flattening the contradiction into a false consensus.

edge_cases.title: Decision pressure exceeds evidence
edge_cases.risk: Stakeholders need a recommendation even though the evidence is still incomplete.
edge_cases.response: Provide the best recommendation available, name the evidence gaps, and state what would change the recommendation.

happy_path.title: Market entry briefing happy path 1
happy_path.prompt: Synthesize analyst notes, competitor pricing snapshots, customer calls, and internal assumptions into a market-entry dossier.
checks: Separate facts, inferences, and assumptions.
Map the strongest signals and the biggest unknowns.
End with a clear recommendation on whether to proceed, pause, or research further.

triggers: You need to design or explain an evaluation benchmark for an agent workflow.
Stakeholders are asking for benchmark results without understanding what the numbers mean.

avoid_when: You need original field research rather than synthesis from available materials.

skills.name: Literature Map
skills.summary: Map a crowded topic into schools of thought, evidence weight, and the shortest smart reading path.
triggers: You need a fast orientation before a team spends a week reading the wrong sources.
The literature is large, repetitive, or internally contested and you need the debate structure first.
expected_inputs: framing question or decision the reading should support
seed bibliography, search results, or must-read source set
constraints on depth, rigor, or turnaround time
skills.description: Use when a topic has too much literature to read linearly and the team needs the structure of the field before deep synthesis.
skills.problem: Teams often burn time reading source by source without first seeing the main schools of thought, the evidence hierarchy, and the disagreements that actually matter to the decision.
use_when: You need a fast orientation before a team spends a week reading the wrong sources.
You need a literature map with theme clusters, representative sources, disputed questions, evidence weight, and a reading order instead of a citation dump.
The work repeats often enough that the reading strategy itself should become reusable.
avoid_when: You need a formal systematic review with explicit inclusion and exclusion methodology.
The answer depends on fresh experiments, interviews, or unpublished internal data rather than existing literature.
skills.deliverable: A literature map with theme clusters, representative sources, evidence weight, disputed questions, and a recommended reading path

scenarios.title: AI evaluation methods scan
scenarios.context: A research and product team needs to understand the main evaluation approaches behind agent benchmarks before committing to one direction.
scenarios.prompt: Map the literature on rubric-based evaluation, model-graded evaluation, and human-review hybrids so the team can decide what to pilot first.
expected: Show the major schools of thought rather than listing papers chronologically.
Distinguish foundational work from recent but thin commentary.
End with a reading path and the key disagreement the team still needs to resolve.

scenarios.title: Board diligence reading plan
scenarios.context: Leadership needs a fast map of a new domain before a board discussion, but nobody has time to read twenty sources end to end.
scenarios.prompt: Produce a literature map on supply-chain automation adoption so leadership can see the main themes, evidence quality, and what to read next.
expected: Group the sources into recognizable themes with representative references.
Call out where the evidence is mature versus where it is still speculative.
Recommend the next three readings that would most change the decision posture.

scenarios.title: Cross-discipline terminology mess
scenarios.context: An internal strategy group is reading healthcare operations, economics, and vendor material that describe the same coordination problem with different language.
scenarios.prompt: Build a literature map on care-team workflow automation where the sources disagree on terminology, study design, and evidence quality.
expected: Normalize the vocabulary without pretending the fields are identical.
Keep evidence quality visible even when the source types vary sharply.
Identify the shortest reading sequence for someone who needs a usable mental model fast.

rubric.strong: Shows the main schools of thought, where they overlap, and the specific fault lines that matter to the reader.
rubric.weak: Produces a long source list that still leaves the debate shapeless.

rubric.strong: Separates seminal, credible, derivative, and speculative sources instead of flattening them into one reading pile.
rubric.weak: Treats every citation as equally informative or equally current.

rubric.strong: Leaves the reader knowing what to read next, what to skip for now, and what question each source will clarify.
rubric.weak: Adds reading volume without improving orientation or decision quality.

edge_cases.title: Citation concentration masks a thin field
edge_cases.risk: The topic looks mature because many papers cite the same foundational work, but fresh evidence is scarce.
edge_cases.response: Call out citation concentration, separate foundational from recycled claims, and lower confidence where newer evidence is weak.

edge_cases.title: Different disciplines use incompatible vocabulary
edge_cases.risk: The same topic is described with different terms across fields, making the literature look more fragmented than it really is.
edge_cases.response: Normalize the terminology, note where the mapping is imperfect, and preserve the concept groups across naming differences.

edge_cases.title: Recency bias distorts importance
edge_cases.risk: Newer articles dominate attention even though older work still frames the core evidence better.
edge_cases.response: Separate recency from analytical weight and explain when an older source still matters more than the latest commentary.

happy_path.title: AI evaluation methods scan happy path
happy_path.prompt: Map the literature on rubric-based evaluation, model-graded evaluation, and human-review hybrids so the team can decide what to pilot first.
checks: Show the major schools of thought rather than listing papers chronologically.
Distinguish foundational work from recent but thin commentary.
End with a reading path and the key disagreement the team still needs to resolve.

happy_path.title: Board diligence reading plan happy path
happy_path.prompt: Produce a literature map on supply-chain automation adoption so leadership can see the main themes, evidence quality, and what to read next.
checks: Group the sources into recognizable themes with representative references.
Call out where the evidence is mature versus where it is still speculative.
Recommend the next three readings that would most change the decision posture.

edge_path.title: Vocabulary mismatch edge path
edge_path.prompt: Run Literature Map when economics papers, operations papers, and vendor reports use different terms for the same underlying workflow problem.
checks: Normalize the terminology without pretending the fields are identical.
Keep the concept groups visible across naming differences.
State where translation between terms may still be imperfect.

edge_path.title: Citation concentration edge path
edge_path.prompt: Run Literature Map when most of the newer sources cite the same few foundational papers and add little independent evidence.
checks: Flag the thinness of fresh evidence instead of calling the field mature.
Separate foundational sources from derivative repetition.
Lower confidence where new data is absent.

failure_cases.title: Chronological reading dump failure probe
failure_cases.prompt: What does a poor Literature Map look like when the source list is long but structurally repetitive?
checks: Identify the failure mode as a source list disguised as synthesis.
Explain why the reader still lacks orientation.
State how the skill should recover by clustering and prioritizing.

failure_cases.title: False consensus failure probe
failure_cases.prompt: What does a poor Literature Map look like when the sources appear aligned only because the analysis erases the real disagreements?
checks: Identify the failure mode as flattening live disagreements into fake agreement.
Explain why that distorts downstream decisions.
State how the skill should surface the contested questions directly.

template_outline: A topic map that groups the literature into themes, evidence strength, and recommended follow-up reading

skills.name: Competitor Briefing
skills.summary: Turn competitor noise into a briefing about intent, durability, and what your team should ignore.
triggers: A competitor announcement, launch, or pricing move is creating internal panic or overreaction.
Leadership needs a brief that separates credible signal from theater before changing roadmap or GTM plans.
expected_inputs: competitor signal set such as launches, pricing changes, win-loss notes, or field reports
your current product or GTM context
skills.description: Use when the team needs a disciplined competitor read, not a screenshot summary or a panic memo.
skills.problem: Competitor analysis often swings between shallow feature comparison and mirror-response panic. Teams need a way to judge what changed, how durable the signal is, and whether any response is actually warranted.
use_when: A competitor announcement, launch, or pricing move is creating internal panic or overreaction.
You need a briefing with signal quality, likely intent, customer relevance, and recommended response posture instead of a feature checklist.
The workflow repeats often enough that the organization benefits from a reusable competitor-reading posture.
avoid_when: You need a formal market sizing exercise or a full strategic planning document.
There is not enough evidence to distinguish signal from rumor and the right move is to wait for more information.
skills.deliverable: A competitor briefing with signal quality, likely strategic intent, customer relevance, and recommended response posture

scenarios.title: Pricing move triggers internal panic
scenarios.context: A direct competitor launches an aggressive entry-level plan and multiple leaders want an immediate response before renewal conversations start slipping.
scenarios.prompt: Create a competitor briefing on the new pricing move that separates durable strategic signal from launch theater and recommends what our team should change now, later, or not at all.
expected: Distinguish customer-relevant signal from headline-level noise.
Explain what the move suggests about intent, not just what changed on the pricing page.
Recommend a response posture that includes the option to do nothing.

scenarios.title: Security narrative shift
scenarios.context: A competitor is suddenly leading with governance and auditability in enterprise deals, and the field team wants to know whether this is messaging, product, or segmentation strategy.
scenarios.prompt: Brief the team on the competitor shift toward governance messaging and spell out whether this changes positioning, product requirements, or enablement priorities for the next quarter.
expected: Separate packaging and narrative changes from substantive product movement.
Tie the competitor move to likely buyer segments and sales situations.
Surface the internal teams that would need to react if the move proves durable.

scenarios.title: Acquisition signal read
scenarios.context: A competitor acquires a workflow tool that overlaps partially with the roadmap, and leadership wants a grounded read before reprioritizing.
scenarios.prompt: Produce a competitor briefing on the acquisition with the likely strategic intent, integration uncertainty, customer relevance, and the minimum response worth considering.
expected: Keep uncertainty explicit around integration timing and execution risk.
Avoid treating the acquisition headline as immediate product reality.
Show the narrowest credible internal response instead of defaulting to roadmap churn.

rubric.strong: Separates durable strategic movement from packaging, rumor, and launch-day theater.
rubric.weak: Treats every new competitor artifact as equally meaningful.

rubric.strong: Protects the team from mirror-response behavior and recommends action only where the evidence justifies it.
rubric.weak: Rewards overreaction and turns every competitor move into internal roadmap turbulence.

rubric.strong: Names which product, GTM, or research responses are warranted now, later, or not at all.
rubric.weak: Ends as commentary with no clear internal posture.

edge_cases.title: Announcement is stronger than the underlying product
edge_cases.risk: The launch narrative is polished, but the actual capability appears thin, early, or hard to verify.
edge_cases.response: Separate messaging strength from product substance and lower confidence until durable evidence appears.

edge_cases.title: The competitor is not really targeting the same segment
edge_cases.risk: Teams overreact because the company name is familiar even though the move primarily targets a different buyer, deployment model, or market tier.
edge_cases.response: Re-center the analysis on actual segment overlap and show where the threat is real, adjacent, or mostly irrelevant.

edge_cases.title: Internal teams want a mirror response
edge_cases.risk: A visible competitor move creates pressure to match features or messaging even when the strategic logic is weak.
edge_cases.response: Make the case for restraint explicit and compare the cost of reaction against the evidence of actual market impact.

happy_path.title: Pricing panic happy path
happy_path.prompt: Create a competitor briefing on the new pricing move that separates durable strategic signal from launch theater and recommends what our team should change now, later, or not at all.
checks: Distinguish customer-relevant signal from headline-level noise.
Explain what the move suggests about intent, not just what changed on the pricing page.
Recommend a response posture that includes the option to do nothing.

happy_path.title: Security narrative shift happy path
happy_path.prompt: Brief the team on the competitor shift toward governance messaging and spell out whether this changes positioning, product requirements, or enablement priorities for the next quarter.
checks: Separate packaging and narrative changes from substantive product movement.
Tie the competitor move to likely buyer segments and sales situations.
Surface the internal teams that would need to react if the move proves durable.

edge_path.title: Vaporware edge path
edge_path.prompt: Run Competitor Briefing when the competitor announcement is polished but the product proof is thin, unclear, or impossible to verify.
checks: Lower confidence instead of repeating the announcement at face value.
Separate narrative power from product substance.
Recommend follow-up evidence to watch for before changing plans.

edge_path.title: Segment mismatch edge path
edge_path.prompt: Run Competitor Briefing when the move looks threatening but appears aimed at a buyer segment we rarely serve.
checks: Test whether the segment overlap is real.
Avoid inflating the internal threat level.
Keep the response proportional to the actual overlap.

failure_cases.title: Feature checklist failure probe
failure_cases.prompt: What does a poor Competitor Briefing look like when the output compares features carefully but says nothing about intent or response posture?
checks: Identify the failure mode as comparison without strategy.
Explain why the team still cannot decide what to do.
State how the skill should reconnect competitor evidence to internal action.

failure_cases.title: Mirror-response failure probe
failure_cases.prompt: What does a poor Competitor Briefing look like when the output assumes every visible competitor move requires an immediate match?
checks: Identify the failure mode as strategic overreaction.
Explain how that distorts roadmap or GTM decisions.
State how the skill should reintroduce evidence thresholds and restraint.

template_outline: A competitor briefing that summarizes moves, interprets likely intent, and recommends internal responses

skills.name: Roadmap Tradeoff Mapper
skills.summary: Compare competing roadmap bets with explicit upside, delay cost, dependency drag, and a forced sequence.
triggers: Two or more roadmap bets are competing for the same quarter and every argument sounds plausible in isolation.
Stakeholders keep ranking ideas without showing what each choice delays, blocks, or makes harder later.
skills.description: Use when prioritization needs explicit cost-of-choice logic instead of another scoring debate.
skills.problem: Roadmap reviews often sound rigorous while hiding the real question: what gets delayed, which dependency becomes gating, and which option still lacks evidence to deserve scarce capacity.
use_when: Two or more roadmap bets are competing for the same quarter and every argument sounds plausible in isolation.
You need a tradeoff map with upside, downside, dependency load, timing cost, and a recommended sequence instead of another ranking ritual.
The organization repeatedly revisits the same priority fights and needs a reusable comparison posture.
avoid_when: The team still lacks credible options worth comparing.
The decision is primarily a budget allocation exercise with little product or dependency tradeoff to reason about.
skills.deliverable: A roadmap tradeoff map with upside, downside, dependency load, delay cost, and a recommended sequence or cut line

scenarios.title: Growth versus reliability quarter
scenarios.context: Product leadership has to choose between a self-serve growth initiative and a reliability program that would reduce churn risk.
scenarios.prompt: Compare the self-serve growth initiative with the reliability program, including dependency load, delay cost, and what evidence is strongest today.
expected: Make the downside of delaying each option explicit.
Show where the same dependency or team capacity constrains both bets.
Force a recommendation instead of leaving the tradeoff abstract.

scenarios.title: Three-way platform tradeoff
scenarios.context: A PM group is debating admin controls, analytics foundation work, and onboarding improvements for the same release window.
scenarios.prompt: Map the tradeoffs across admin controls, analytics foundation work, and onboarding improvements so the team can decide sequence and cuts.
expected: Compare the options on the same dimensions instead of changing criteria midstream.
Surface which option creates future leverage versus immediate user-visible value.
Recommend sequence or cuts with explicit reasoning.

scenarios.title: Enterprise ask versus core retention
scenarios.context: An enterprise feature request could unlock a visible account, but the same quarter also contains a retention bet for the broader base.
scenarios.prompt: Build a tradeoff map between the enterprise request and the core retention initiative, including dependency conflicts, customer concentration risk, and what delaying either choice really costs.
expected: Show the concentration risk in chasing one account-heavy bet.
Model dependency conflicts instead of assuming parallel execution.
Make the recommendation survive executive scrutiny, not just team preference.

rubric.strong: Compares options on the same dimensions so the tradeoff is real rather than rhetorical.
rubric.weak: Changes evaluation criteria until every option sounds defensible.

rubric.strong: Shows where shared teams, platform work, or timing constraints actually shape the decision.
rubric.weak: Mentions dependencies without letting them change the recommendation.

rubric.strong: Leaves the team with a recommendation, sequence, or explicit cut line.
rubric.weak: Produces a balanced memo that still avoids choosing.

edge_cases.title: Every option leans on the same bottleneck team
edge_cases.risk: A platform, data, or security team is a shared dependency, making all bets look independently feasible when they are not.
edge_cases.response: Model the bottleneck explicitly and show how it changes sequencing, not just desirability.

edge_cases.title: Political favorite has weak evidence
edge_cases.risk: One option has executive support but thinner user or business evidence than the alternatives.
edge_cases.response: Keep the evidence gap visible and state what would justify the favored option more credibly.

edge_cases.title: Cost estimates are uneven
edge_cases.risk: One option has detailed sizing while another remains rough, which can distort the comparison.
edge_cases.response: Note the asymmetry, normalize confidence where possible, and avoid false precision.

skills.name: Feature Spec Refiner
skills.summary: Tighten a draft spec until scope, state changes, acceptance criteria, and handoff risk are explicit.
triggers: A spec is nearly ready for handoff, but engineers or designers keep finding ambiguity in the details.
The team has decided to build the feature, yet the document still hides assumptions that will surface during implementation.
skills.description: Use when a spec looks close to done but is still too loose to hand to design or engineering safely.
skills.problem: Specs fail less often because of typos than because they smuggle in ambiguous states, hidden dependencies, and acceptance criteria nobody could actually test.
use_when: A spec is nearly ready for handoff, but engineers or designers keep finding ambiguity in the details.
You need a refined spec with explicit scope, assumptions, state changes, and testable acceptance criteria instead of a polished but slippery document.
The team repeats this handoff problem often enough that the review posture should be reusable.
avoid_when: The team is still deciding whether the feature should exist at all.
The artifact is actually strategy or discovery work rather than a build-ready specification.
skills.deliverable: A refined feature spec with explicit scope boundaries, state changes, assumptions, and testable acceptance criteria

scenarios.title: Notification batching handoff
scenarios.context: A PM wrote a batching spec for notifications, but engineering is already flagging ambiguity around timing, fallback behavior, and user controls.
scenarios.prompt: Refine the notification batching spec so scope boundaries, fallback behavior, and acceptance criteria are clear enough for implementation planning.
expected: Clarify what is in scope now versus explicitly deferred.
Make fallback behavior and edge conditions visible.
Convert vague success statements into testable acceptance criteria.

scenarios.title: Permissions expansion spec
scenarios.context: A team is extending a permission model and needs a cleaner spec before design and engineering finalize the build plan.
scenarios.prompt: Tighten the permissions expansion spec so role boundaries, dependency assumptions, and acceptance criteria are explicit.
expected: Expose hidden cross-team or data dependencies.
State the assumption list instead of burying it in prose.
Make boundary conditions around roles and overrides testable.

scenarios.title: Audit-log export workflow
scenarios.context: A compliance-driven export feature is specified at a high level, but nobody has pinned down state transitions, failure handling, or ownership for long-running jobs.
scenarios.prompt: Refine the audit-log export spec so request states, failure handling, ownership boundaries, and acceptance criteria are implementation-ready.
expected: Make the state model and transitions explicit.
Surface cross-team dependencies such as storage, security review, or admin UX.
Leave engineering with acceptance criteria they could turn into test cases immediately.

rubric.strong: Distinguishes current scope, deferred scope, and assumptions instead of leaving boundaries implied.
rubric.weak: Uses polished language while leaving the actual build boundary fuzzy.

rubric.strong: Surfaces the states, dependencies, ownership questions, and missing decisions that would block implementation.
rubric.weak: Reads cleanly but still leaves implementers to discover the hard parts later.

rubric.strong: Turns success criteria into concrete conditions another team could test.
rubric.weak: Uses aspirational success language that no one could verify consistently.

edge_cases.title: Design is still fluid
edge_cases.risk: Key interaction details remain unsettled, which can make the spec sound more final than it really is.
edge_cases.response: Separate what is stable from what still depends on design resolution and avoid pretending the handoff is complete.

edge_cases.title: Hidden cross-team dependency
edge_cases.risk: A seemingly contained feature depends on another service, policy decision, or shared workflow that the spec barely mentions.
edge_cases.response: Pull the dependency into the main structure and show how it affects scope, timing, or acceptance criteria.

edge_cases.title: Stakeholders ask for wording cleanup only
edge_cases.risk: The team wants a cleaner document without surfacing the real ambiguity that will break implementation.
edge_cases.response: Treat clarity as a means to expose missing decisions, not as a cosmetic editing pass.

checks: Make the state model and transitions explicit.

skills.summary: Review an API change for silent behavior shifts, consumer blast radius, and rollout realism.
triggers: An API shape or default is changing and downstream consumers may break or behave differently.

skills.description: Use when an API change needs a contract review that goes beyond the diff.

use_when: An API shape or default is changing and downstream consumers may break or behave differently.
You need a review with compatibility analysis, hidden behavioral change detection, blast-radius reasoning, and rollout guidance instead of a code review summary.
The organization repeats this pattern enough that the review logic should be reusable.
avoid_when: You only need schema linting or code-generation checks.
The contract is still entirely private and there are no meaningful consumers yet.

scenarios.prompt: Review the proposed deprecation of a public response field and summarize compatibility risk, likely consumer breakage, and the minimum safe migration path.
expected: Separate hard breaking changes from behavior changes that are only likely to break some clients.
Call out undocumented consumer assumptions such as nullability or default parsing.
Recommend rollout and communication steps that match the blast radius.

scenarios.title: Pagination default change
scenarios.context: An internal service wants to change pagination defaults to reduce load, but several downstream consumers may rely on current behavior.
scenarios.prompt: Review the pagination default change for compatibility, consumer impact, and rollout sequencing.
expected: Treat default behavior as part of the contract, not an implementation detail.
Show which consumers are likely to feel the change first.
Recommend a rollout path safer than flipping the default globally.

scenarios.title: Webhook retry semantics shift
scenarios.context: A platform team wants to change retry timing and idempotency guidance for webhook delivery, and support is worried about duplicate downstream actions.
scenarios.prompt: Review the webhook retry semantics change for contract risk, hidden consumer assumptions, and the safest migration posture.
expected: Treat timing and retry behavior as contract, not implementation detail.
Call out downstream systems likely to duplicate work or mis-handle retries.
Tie the rollout plan to observability and support readiness.

rubric.strong: Identifies explicit schema breaks and quieter behavioral shifts that consumers still experience as contract changes.
rubric.weak: Looks only for obvious type or field removals.

rubric.strong: Maps which consumers are affected, how badly, and where assumptions are undocumented.
rubric.weak: Talks about consumers generically without narrowing the impact.

rubric.strong: Pairs the review with deprecation, migration, communication, and support steps that match the risk.
rubric.weak: Finds issues but leaves rollout strategy naive or missing.

edge_cases.title: Versioning promises more safety than reality

skills.summary: Design an operating rhythm with clear decision cadence, artifact discipline, and escalation paths.
triggers: The team has too many meetings, too little decision clarity, or too much status noise between leaders.
An organization is scaling past founder or ad hoc coordination and needs a repeatable operating cadence.
expected_inputs: recurring decisions that need cadence support
skills.description: Use when the problem is not just too many meetings, but weak decision cadence and poor operating discipline.

use_when: The team has too many meetings, too little decision clarity, or too much status noise between leaders.
You need a rhythm with cadence, artifacts, owners, and escalation paths instead of another calendar cleanup exercise.

avoid_when: The organization is too early to define recurring decisions or owners at all.
The actual problem is leadership conflict or missing authority, not cadence design.

scenarios.title: Founder-led startup scaling up

scenarios.prompt: Design an operating rhythm for a scaling startup with weekly execution reviews, monthly planning, and explicit escalation handling across product, engineering, and GTM.
expected: Define which decisions belong in which cadence rather than stacking more meetings.
Specify the artifact each forum requires to stay useful.
Make escalation paths explicit when a decision cannot wait for the next scheduled review.

scenarios.title: Distributed engineering and product cadence
scenarios.context: A distributed organization has metrics arriving late, duplicated reviews, and recurring disagreement about where prioritization decisions should happen.
scenarios.prompt: Create an operating rhythm that reduces duplicated forums, clarifies decision ownership, and matches meeting cadence to data freshness.
expected: Match cadence to how quickly meaningful signal actually arrives.
Collapse redundant forums instead of renaming them.
Clarify who decides, who prepares the artifact, and when escalation is triggered.

scenarios.title: Post-incident operating reset
scenarios.context: After several avoidable escalations, leadership wants to redesign the weekly operating system so risks surface earlier and owners do not improvise under pressure.
scenarios.prompt: Design an operating rhythm that improves weekly signal review, issue escalation, and owner discipline after repeated cross-functional misses.
expected: Tie the rhythm to the specific operating failures that keep recurring.

Avoid creating a ceremony-heavy model that nobody can sustain.

rubric.strong: Matches each recurring forum to the type and speed of decisions it should support.
rubric.weak: Creates calendar structure without improving decision timing.

rubric.strong: Names the artifact, owner, and expected level of preparation required for each cadence point.
rubric.weak: Treats meetings as sufficient without defining the inputs that make them useful.

rubric.weak: Mentions escalation but leaves the actual route ambiguous.

edge_cases.title: Metrics arrive too late for the proposed cadence

edge_cases.title: No stable owner exists for a key artifact

skills.name: Deployment Readiness Review
skills.summary: Review whether a release is actually ready to roll out, observe, and reverse if needed.
triggers: A service or feature is approaching release and the team needs a real go or no-go read instead of optimism.
The launch path depends on rollout controls, monitoring, rollback posture, or operational coordination that could still fail.
skills.description: Use when a release needs a disciplined readiness review, not a ceremonial checklist pass.
skills.problem: Teams confuse launch confidence with launch readiness. A release is only ready if the rollout, monitoring, rollback, and operator handoff are credible under the actual risk profile.
use_when: A service or feature is approaching release and the team needs a real go or no-go read instead of optimism.
You need a readiness review with gating logic, rollout realism, monitoring posture, and rollback credibility instead of a checklist that everyone wants to pass.
The organization repeats launch decisions often enough that the review stance should be reusable.
avoid_when: The change is still too early for release planning and basic implementation facts are missing.
You only need a deployment procedure, not a go or no-go review.
skills.deliverable: A deployment readiness review with gating logic, rollout plan, monitoring posture, rollback credibility, and unresolved risks

scenarios.title: Feature-flagged release with migration risk
scenarios.context: A feature is technically behind a flag, but the release still includes a migration and changes to support workflows.
scenarios.prompt: Review this feature-flagged launch for real deployment readiness, including whether the flag meaningfully reduces risk, what still needs monitoring, and what should block rollout.
expected: Treat the flag as one control, not as automatic proof of safe rollout.
Surface migration and support-readiness risk alongside code readiness.
State what should block rollout if unresolved.

scenarios.title: Mobile backend rollout
scenarios.context: A backend change will affect a mobile client population that updates slowly, so rollback and observability posture matter more than usual.
scenarios.prompt: Produce a deployment readiness review for the backend rollout with client-version risk, monitoring posture, and rollback realism.
expected: Account for mixed client populations and delayed upgrades.
Tie monitoring to the failure modes most likely to surface first.
Be honest about what rollback can and cannot undo once traffic shifts.

scenarios.title: Data-pipeline upgrade under time pressure
scenarios.context: A data-platform team wants to ship an upgrade before quarter close, but monitoring coverage and rollback clarity are both weaker than they should be.
scenarios.prompt: Review the readiness of the data-pipeline upgrade, including gating checks, monitoring gaps, on-call posture, and whether the launch should slip.
expected: Use go or no-go language instead of vague risk commentary.
Make monitoring and on-call readiness first-class gates.
Explain when slipping the launch is the stronger operational decision.

rubric.strong: Defines which unresolved items block rollout and which can be accepted knowingly.
rubric.weak: Lists risks without turning them into go or no-go logic.

rubric.strong: Treats flags, staged rollout, and rollback as operational tools with real limits.
rubric.weak: Assumes controls exist in theory and therefore solve the problem.

rubric.strong: Connects launch readiness to the ability to detect, interpret, and respond to failure quickly.
rubric.weak: Mentions monitoring without tying it to the actual release risk.

edge_cases.title: Feature flag is mistaken for rollback
edge_cases.risk: The team treats a flag as equivalent to rollback even though data changes, side effects, or support impact are not reversible.
edge_cases.response: Separate traffic control from true rollback and make the residual risk explicit.

edge_cases.title: Migration is hard to reverse
edge_cases.risk: A schema or data migration introduces irreversible or high-cost rollback conditions.
edge_cases.response: Treat migration reversibility as a first-class gate and avoid approving rollout on flag logic alone.

edge_cases.title: On-call is not briefed
edge_cases.risk: The release team is ready, but the operators who will absorb the failure modes are not prepared.
edge_cases.response: Include on-call briefing, support posture, and escalation readiness in the release gate instead of treating them as post-launch details.

happy_path.title: Feature-flagged release with migration risk happy path

checks: Treat the flag as one control, not as automatic proof of safe rollout.

State what should block rollout if unresolved.

checks: Use go or no-go language instead of vague risk commentary.

skills.name: Tool Contract Auditor
skills.summary: Audit a tool contract for ambiguous parameters, missing guarantees, and agent-hostile failure behavior.
triggers: A tool or API is meant for agents, but the contract still reads like it was designed only for humans.
The workflow depends on a tool whose inputs, outputs, or failure semantics are underspecified.
expected_inputs: examples of current usage if available
skills.description: Use when a tool contract needs to survive agent use, retries, and machine interpretation instead of relying on human guesswork.
skills.problem: Tools often look workable until an agent hits ambiguous parameters, unclear partial-success states, or retry behavior that can cause duplicate or destructive actions.
use_when: A tool or API is meant for agents, but the contract still reads like it was designed only for humans.
You need an audit of parameter semantics, failure semantics, and safe retry behavior instead of a schema review alone.
The organization will reuse the tool enough that contract quality has long-tail impact on agent reliability.
avoid_when: The tool is still too early to have meaningful contract expectations.
You only need implementation debugging rather than contract analysis.
skills.deliverable: A tool contract audit with ambiguity findings, failure and retry risks, and recommended contract fixes

scenarios.title: Search tool with ambiguous filters
scenarios.context: An internal search tool works for humans in a UI, but the API contract leaves filters, defaults, and sort behavior too vague for reliable agent use.
scenarios.prompt: Audit the search tool contract for ambiguous parameter semantics, default behavior, and the changes required to make it safer for agent use.
expected: Treat vague defaults and overloaded parameters as contract defects, not minor wording issues.
Explain how ambiguity would show up during agent execution.
Recommend fixes that narrow machine interpretation.

scenarios.title: Ticket-creation tool with partial success
scenarios.context: A ticketing integration can create a record, attach files, and notify watchers, but the contract does not say what happens if one stage fails.
scenarios.prompt: Review the ticket-creation tool contract with emphasis on partial-success handling, retry safety, and result semantics.
expected: Make partial-success states explicit instead of assuming all-or-nothing behavior.
Review whether retries could duplicate or corrupt work.
Recommend clearer result contracts for downstream agents.

scenarios.title: Billing action with dangerous retries
scenarios.context: A financial operations tool exposes a mutation endpoint whose retry guidance is unclear, raising the risk of duplicate charges or repeated credits.
scenarios.prompt: Audit the billing-action tool contract for retry safety, idempotency expectations, and agent-safe failure semantics.
expected: Treat retry behavior as a core contract property, not an implementation footnote.
Surface where agent automation could amplify damage.
Recommend the minimum guarantees needed before agent use is safe.

rubric.strong: Finds fields, defaults, or overloaded parameters that a machine could interpret inconsistently.
rubric.weak: Confuses human readability with machine-safe semantics.

rubric.strong: Makes success, partial success, retriable failure, and terminal failure states explicit.
rubric.weak: Treats all non-success outcomes as one vague error bucket.

rubric.strong: Connects contract defects to concrete automation risk such as duplicate actions, unsafe retries, or hidden side effects.
rubric.weak: Calls the contract unclear without showing why the ambiguity is dangerous in agent workflows.

edge_cases.title: Partial success is undefined
edge_cases.risk: A tool performs multiple side effects, but the contract does not say how mixed outcomes are represented.
edge_cases.response: Make partial-success states explicit and recommend result shapes that allow agents to recover safely.

edge_cases.title: Fields are human-friendly but machine-ambiguous
edge_cases.risk: Parameters use labels or defaults that a human can guess correctly, but an agent may interpret inconsistently.
edge_cases.response: Recommend narrower schemas, explicit enums, and defaults that are safe under automation.

edge_cases.title: Retry semantics can cause harm
edge_cases.risk: The contract implies retries are acceptable without defining idempotency or duplicate-effect behavior.
edge_cases.response: Treat retry behavior as a safety-critical contract dimension and flag unsafe automation paths clearly.

happy_path.title: Ambiguous search tool happy path

checks: Treat vague defaults and overloaded parameters as contract defects, not minor wording issues.
Explain how ambiguity would show up during agent execution.
Recommend fixes that narrow machine interpretation.

happy_path.title: Billing action retry safety happy path
happy_path.prompt: Audit the billing-action tool contract for retry safety, idempotency expectations, and agent-safe failure semantics.
checks: Treat retry behavior as a core contract property, not an implementation footnote.
Surface where agent automation could amplify damage.
Recommend the minimum guarantees needed before agent use is safe.

edge_path.title: Partial success edge path
edge_path.prompt: Run Tool Contract Auditor when a tool can create records, attach files, and send notifications, but the contract says nothing about mixed outcomes.
checks: Make partial-success states explicit.
Connect mixed outcomes to recovery behavior.
Recommend result semantics agents can act on safely.

edge_path.title: Human-friendly ambiguity edge path
edge_path.prompt: Run Tool Contract Auditor when parameters are understandable to humans in the UI but ambiguous for machine callers.
checks: Treat human-friendly labels as insufficient.
Recommend narrower schemas or enums.
Explain how ambiguity would create agent errors.

failure_cases.title: Schema-only audit failure probe
failure_cases.prompt: What does a poor Tool Contract Auditor output look like when it reviews field names but ignores partial success and retry semantics?
checks: Identify the failure mode as schema-only review.
Explain why agent risk remains high.
State how the skill should expand into execution and recovery semantics.

failure_cases.title: Unsafe retry failure probe
failure_cases.prompt: What does a poor Tool Contract Auditor output look like when it treats retries as harmless without checking idempotency or duplicate side effects?
checks: Identify the failure mode as unsafe retry assumptions.
Explain how agent automation can amplify the defect.
State how the skill should make retry guarantees explicit.
